coredata.dc:title: Hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene as a vector sensing and delivery of Carmustine anticancer drug: A density functional theory study
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Dolatabad, Amin Amiri
Salehpour, Mahboobeh
Saadati, Zohreh
coredata.dc:description: We conducted research on the adsorption of carmustine (BCNU) on the surface of pristine, N-doped, and BN-doped Hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene (HBC) nanographene using density functional theory calculations. We used the B3LYP functional in conjunction with 6-31+G(d) and 6-311+G(d,p) basis sets to perform geometrical optimization calculations, vibrational frequencies, and natural bond orbitals (NBO) analysis. The dispersion correction term of Grimme-D3 was incorporated to account for the dispersion interactions. We found that the geometry of the nanographenes remains unchanged after the drug molecule adsorption. The calculated adsorption energies (E ads) were −17.1 kcal.mol−1 for HBC-BCNU, −17.0 kcal.mol−1 for NHBC-BCNU, and −17.9 kcal.mol−1 for BNHBC-BCNU when using the 6-31+G(d) basis set. The corresponding values calculated employing the 6-311+G(d,p) basis set were −17.9, −17.3, and −19.1 kcal.mol−1, respectively. Analysis of frontier molecular orbitals (HOMO and LUMO) showed that the electronic properties of NHBC were significantly affected by the presence of BCNU. We used NBO analysis to investigate the distribution of partial atomic charges in different systems. Finally, using the transition state equation and the smaller basis set, we found that the required time for BCNU to release from HBC, NHBC, and BNHBC is approximately 3.3, 1.8, and 13.0 s, respectively. The obtained values are 13.0, 4.6, and 96.0 s, respectively, for the higher level of calculations. The recovery time for all nanographenes is within a reasonable range, while NHBC exhibits the highest sensitivity for drug detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanographene
Hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene
Doping technique
Carmustine
Drug delivery
Biosensors

Adsorption of BCNU on the N-doped HBC
Adsorption of BCNU on the BN-doped HBC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized structure of (a) HBC and (b) BCNU. Computed molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) plot of (c) HBC and (d) BCNU (The surfaces are defined by the 0.0004 electrons/b3 contour of the electronic density. Color ranges, in a.u.: blue, more positive than 0.010; green, between 0.010 and 0; yellow, between 0 and −0.010; red, more negative than −0.010). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Optimized structure and (b) PDOS plot of HBC-BCNU complex.
The HOMO and LUMO profile of the HBC-BCNU complex.

Atomic charge distribution of HBC and HBC-BCNU. Negative charges are represented by red, neutral charges by black, and positive charges by green. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Optimized structure and (b) Computed MEP plot of the NHBC.
(a) Optimized structure and (b) PDOS plot of the NHBC-BCNU complex.
The HOMO and LUMO profile of the NHBC-BCNU complex.

Atomic charge distribution of NHBC and NHBC-BCNU. Negative charges are represented by red, neutral charges by black, and positive charges by green. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Optimized structure and (b) Computed MEP plot of BNHBC.
(a) Optimized structure and (b) PDOS plot of BNHBC-BCNU complex.
The HOMO and LUMO profile of the BNHBC-BCNU complex.

Atomic charge distribution of HBC and BNHBC-BCNU. Negative charges are represented by red, neutral charges by black, and positive charges by green. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Weak interaction regions and NCI plots of HBC-BCNU, NHBC-BCNU, and BNHBC-BCNU complexes.
Calculated energetic data (ΔH, ΔG, and E ads in kcal.mol−1 for (N/BN)HBC-BCNU complexes.
Calculated HOMO and LUMO energies (E HOMO and E LUMO in eV) and HOMO-LUMO energy gap (E g) for the structures under investigation.

head.ce:title: Hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene as a vector sensing and delivery of Carmustine anticancer drug: A density functional theory study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Omidiyeh Branch, Islamic Azad University, Omidiyeh, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Omidiyeh Branch, Islamic Azad University, Omidiyeh, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We conducted research on the adsorption of carmustine (BCNU) on the surface of pristine, N-doped, and BN-doped Hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene (HBC) nanographene using density functional theory calculations. We used the B3LYP functional in conjunction with 6-31+G(d) and 6-311+G(d,p) basis sets to perform geometrical optimization calculations, vibrational frequencies, and natural bond orbitals (NBO) analysis. The dispersion correction term of Grimme-D3 was incorporated to account for the dispersion interactions. We found that the geometry of the nanographenes remains unchanged after the drug molecule adsorption. The calculated adsorption energies (E ads) were −17.1 kcal.mol−1 for HBC-BCNU, −17.0 kcal.mol−1 for NHBC-BCNU, and −17.9 kcal.mol−1 for BNHBC-BCNU when using the 6-31+G(d) basis set. The corresponding values calculated employing the 6-311+G(d,p) basis set were −17.9, −17.3, and −19.1 kcal.mol−1, respectively. Analysis of frontier molecular orbitals (HOMO and LUMO) showed that the electronic properties of NHBC were significantly affected by the presence of BCNU. We used NBO analysis to investigate the distribution of partial atomic charges in different systems. Finally, using the transition state equation and the smaller basis set, we found that the required time for BCNU to release from HBC, NHBC, and BNHBC is approximately 3.3, 1.8, and 13.0 s, respectively. The obtained values are 13.0, 4.6, and 96.0 s, respectively, for the higher level of calculations. The recovery time for all nanographenes is within a reasonable range, while NHBC exhibits the highest sensitivity for drug detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carmustine (BCNU) is a chemotherapy drug used to treat brain tumors. However, it is associated with severe side effects such as pulmonary fibrosis and liver toxicity [1,2]. Also, BCNU may produce drug resistance in tumor cells and limit their applications in clinical treatment, so new strategies are necessary to improve its therapeutic effect [3,4]. A study proposed that 2-Deoxy-D-glucose, a glycolytic inhibitor that can reprogram tumor energy metabolism, may have the potential to increase the sensitivity of glioblastoma cells to BCNU by regulating glycolysis, reactive oxygen species, and endoplasmic reticulum stress pathways in clinical setting [5]. Similarly, BCNU is often ineffective due to tumor cells' ability to repair DNA damage. Inhibition of aerobic glycolysis may be an attractive strategy to overcome this resistance and improve the chemotherapeutic effects of BCNU. Sun et al. discussed the use of 3-bromopyruvate (3-BrPA) as a potential chemosensitizer to optimize the therapeutic index of BCNU. The study investigated the effects of 3-BrPA on two human hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) cell lines and found that it significantly increased their sensitivity to BCNU. The results also showed that 3-BrPA decreased cellular ATP and GSH levels, extracellular lactate excreted by tumor cells, and cellular hexokinase-II (HK-II) activity, making it a potential clinical chemosensitizer [6]. Another approach to solve this issue is the development of new drug delivery systems based on nanotechnology [7–9]. Pharmaceutical carriers increase drug solubility in aqueous media, reduce side effects, and maintain basic pharmacophoric properties [10]. Various nanocarriers, such as liposomes [11], polymeric micelles [12], carbon nanotubes [13–15], dendrimers [16], and two-dimensional nanostructures [17,18] are used for stable and controlled drug delivery. Each of these carriers has its advantages, such as the ability to maintain drug concentration at a constant level for a certain period, adjust the drug release rate depending on the delivery site, deliver drugs to specific organs or tissues, and deliver multiple drugs with a specific formulation to the target cell.

Graphene, a two-dimensional nanostructure, is known for its unique properties like high surface area, stability, strength, and flexibility [19]. However, pristine graphene behaves as a semi-metal with zero energy gap and linear band dispersion at the Fermi level, indicating that there are still fundamental challenges in the design and development of graphene-based sensors. To address this, researchers suggest chemical modifications and nanographene formation [20–22]. Nanographenes are zero-dimensional structures with special electronic and optical properties suitable for various applications. They can be synthesized precisely using bottom-up methods [23]. Nanographenes offer high surface areas for drug delivery, biocompatibility, and low toxicity, making them ideal for catalysis, sensing, and optoelectronics [23,24]. Some examples of nanographenes include coronene, hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene (HBC), graphene nanoribbons (GNRs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). However, more research is needed to fully understand their properties and potential applications [25,26]. HBC (C42H18) is a flat and disk-like structure composed of thirteen fused benzene rings arranged in a hexagonal pattern, making it a planar polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon. Due to its facile synthetic route, HBC is a highly promising candidate for therapeutic applications. HBC has a high degree of conjugation because of the delocalization of electrons in its benzene rings, and it has an approximate diameter of 1.2 nm and a thickness of 0.4 nm. It can be obtained through various methods, including cyclodehydrogenation of precursor molecules or oxidative coupling of aromatic compounds. The most common method of HBC synthesis involves the reaction between hexafluorobenzene and a dibrominated benzene derivative catalyzed by palladium [27]. HBC has several physicochemical properties such as high thermal stability (its decomposition temperature is about 500°C), excellent electronic conductivity, and strong absorption in the visible and near-infrared regions, which make it an ideal material for potential applications in electronics, optoelectronics, and materials science. HBC is very soluble in organic solvents such as chloroform and toluene, and it has a high surface area and can form self-assembled monolayers on different substrates.

HBC can be used to make organic semiconductors, organic field effect transistors (OFET), organic light-emitting diodes (OLED), and organic photovoltaics (OPV). Also, materials based on HBC have been investigated as electrocatalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction in fuel cells [28–30]. Although HBC has been used as a fluorescent probe for imaging and bioassay applications, it has not been extensively investigated for biomedical applications [31–34]. However, it has been shown that HBC can be functionalized with different chemical groups to improve its solubility and biocompatibility and enable targeted drug delivery [35–37]. HBCs can be used as carriers for a wide range of drugs, including anticancer drugs, anti-inflammatory agents, and antibiotics. It has also been shown that graphene quantum dots have low toxicity and good biocompatibility, which makes them promising candidates for drug delivery applications [38,39]. Several experimental and theoretical studies have been conducted to assess the effectiveness of using HBC in biomedical applications. For example, Song et al. synthesized Fe3O4/HBC@F127 nanocomposites for dual-mode imaging (fluorescent-MR) and drug delivery. These nanocomposites showed good fluorescence and MR imaging effects with low cellular toxicity, making them a promising candidate for use as drug delivery carriers and as a substrate for dual-mode imaging [33]. Another study used DFT calculations to investigate the interaction between the drug fluorouracil (FU) and HBC, as well as its doped derivatives. The study found that doping HBC with AlN and AlP significantly increases its ability to adsorb the FU drug. The results suggested that nanographenes doped with AlN and AlP hold great potential as carriers for delivering the FU drug [37]. Liu et al. investigated the effectiveness of central ring doped HBC as gamma-butyrolactone (GBL) sensors using the DFT method. They found that GBL is adsorbed on top of BN and AlN rings, with adsorption energy values of −20.0 and −44.1 kcal.mol−1, respectively. The HOMO-LUMO energy gap (E g) of BN-HBC remains unchanged after GBL adsorption, while the E g of AlN-HBC decreases by about 33.5 %. It was revealed that 1,4-butanediol does not affect the detection of GBL by AlN-HBC, but Gamma-hydroxybutyrate is an interfering molecule [36]. This study investigates the potential of HBC as a nanocarrier for BCNU using density functional theory.

All calculations will be performed using the DFT method and the B3LYP functional, along with the 6-31+G(d) and 6-311+G(d,p) basis sets in the Gaussian 09 medium [40]. B3LYP is a reliable method that offers good performance and reasonable computing resources. It is frequently used to study the physicochemical properties of nanostructures [41]. However, it has been shown that the B3LYP functional is not precise enough to explain noncovalent interactions [42]. To address this issue, we incorporated the dispersion correction term of Grimme-D3 to account for the dispersion interactions [43]. To create the nanographenes and BCNU structures, we used the GaussView software [44]. To confirm that the optimized structures are the minimum points on the potential energy surface, and not a saddle point or a transition state, we also performed vibrational frequency calculations. These calculations also allowed us to obtain zero point energy corrections (ZPE) and parameters such as enthalpy changes (ΔH) and Gibbs free energy changes (ΔG). The ΔH value due to BCNU adsorption on the HBC surface at a temperature of 298 K and a pressure of 1 atm, was obtained using the following equation:
(1)
Δ H =
Δ H
Complex
-
Δ H
Nanographene
-
Δ H
Drug
Gibbs free energy changes (ΔG) caused by the complex formation between BCNU molecule and pristine or doped HBC (E ads) were calculated using the corresponding data under the same temperature and pressure. The implicit solvent effect was applied through the self-consistent reaction field (SCRF) approach and utilizing the polarizable continuum model (PCM) considering the water dielectric constant (ε = 78.36). To gain a deeper understanding of the charge transfer process between carrier and drug molecules, we performed natural bond orbital (NBO) calculations to analyze the charge distribution of the (N/BN)HBC-BCNU complex [45]. The energy gap (E g) between the highest occupied molecule orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of (N/BN)HBC before and after the complex formation is calculated using the following equation:
(2)
E g
=
E
LUMO
-
E
HOMO
The partial density of states (DOS) were plotted using Multiwfn software [46]. The electronic sensitivity of pristine and doped HBC towards the BCNU drug is calculated using the following equation:
(3)
Δ
E g
=
E g
Complex
-
E g
Nanographene
E g
Nanographene
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of BCNU on the pristine HBC
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized geometry and molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) maps of HBC and BCNU [47,48] molecules are presented in Fig. 1 . The structure of HBC comprises of 13 fused benzene rings that are arranged in a hexagonal pattern, creating a flat and disk-like shape. The benzene rings in HBC can be categorized into three types- 1 central ring (Rc), 6 internal rings (Ri), and 6 outer rings (Ro). The figure also highlights five different types of bonds − numbered 1 to 5 − with corresponding bond lengths of about 1.39 Å, 1.40 Å, 1.46 Å, 1.45 Å, and 1.42 Å, respectively, calculated at B3LYP/6-31+G(d,p). These bond lengths remain unchanged when the 6-311+G(d,p) basis set is used. These bond lengths are consistent with the values reported in the literature [36,49]. Overall, the figure provides a comprehensive understanding of the molecular structure of HBC, which is essential in its characterization and further studies.

To determine the most energetically stable configuration of the HBC-BCNU complex, we utilized the molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) map of the drug and nanographene. The MEP map displays the molecular level with different colors, with red representing electron-rich regions, blue representing electron-deficient regions, and yellow and green representing intermediate potentials. We investigated all possible orientations for placing BCNU on the HBC surface. We performed structural optimization on all HBC-BCNU initial configurations and calculated the adsorption energies (E ads) for each of them. To ensure that the optimized structures correspond to the true minima on the potential energy surface and to calculate the zero point energy (E ZPE), we performed vibrational frequency calculations. Finally, we selected the complex with the most negative energy value and all its vibrational modes had positive values as the most stable HBC-BCNU complex to be further investigated.

Table 1 shows the values of ΔH, ΔG, and E ads of the most stable HBC-BCNU configuration at 298 K and 1 atm in terms of kcal.mol−1, which are −20.0, −4.6, and −17.1, respectively, using the 6-31+G(d) basis set. By employing the 6-31++G(d,p) basis set, the ΔH, ΔG, and E ads values are calculated to be –22.9, −4.7, and −17.9 kcal.mol−1, respectively. The basis set superposition error values are also reported in Table 1. These calculated values indicate strong interactions between HBC and BCNU. However, it is important to note that such a large adsorption energy may prevent the release of the drug. The interaction between a nanocarrier and a drug should not be so strong that it is impossible to release the drug when it reaches the target cells; on the other hand, weak adsorption energies can lead to drug release in an unfavorable environment.

Fig. 2 shows the structure of the most stable HBC-BCNU complex and the corresponding PDOS plot. The geometrical changes of HBC and BCNU after the interaction are insignificant, indicating that despite the strong interaction, bond lengths and bond angles remain almost constant.

Table 2 presents the values of E g for the structures under investigation. Upon adsorption of BCNU on the HBC surface, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels experience an increase in stability by 0.11 eV and 0.33 eV, respectively. This leads to a minor decrease of only −6.2 % in E g. Therefore, it can be inferred that the electronic properties of HBC are relatively unresponsive to the presence of the BCNU molecule. The results obtained by employing the 6-311+G(d,p) basis set confirm these results. The PDOS plot (Fig. 2(b)) computed for the HBC-BCNU complex illustrates that HBC is primarily responsible for determining the HOMO energy level in the complex, while the nanographene and drug contribute to determining the LUMO energy. The distribution of HOMO and LUMO orbitals in the HBC-BCNU structure, as shown in Fig. 3 , is consistent with the PDOS diagram.

Fig. 4 illustrates the atomic charge distribution of HBC and HBC-BCNU, utilizing color codes to indicate the respective charges of each atom. Negative charges are represented by red, neutral charges by black, and positive charges by green. The depiction in Fig. 4(a) reveals that carbon atoms in the HBC molecule bonded with hydrogen atoms have a more negative atomic charge than other carbon atoms. In contrast, carbon atoms that form type 3 bonds carry the most positive partial charges, indicated by the green color. The partial charge of edge carbon atoms is the most negative, ranging from −0.992 to −0.352 |e|. The internal carbon atoms of HBC exhibit an atomic charge close to zero, while the partial charge of the most positive carbon atoms ranges from 0.598 to 0.604 |e|. Fig. 4(b) depicts that the edge carbon atoms and hydrogen atoms continue to exhibit the most negative and positive partial charges, respectively. The NBO calculations suggest that only a negligible amount of charge transfer occurs from HBC to the drug.

The changes in the energy gap between HOMO and LUMO and the charge transfer between HBC and BCNU are insignificant. To modify the structure of nanographene for our purposes, we need to make changes to it. One effective method to improve the desirable chemical properties of carbon-based materials is through doping. Doped atoms change the energy and structural parameters of the system, while also providing more interaction sites on the surface of nanostructures. Therefore, we have decided to use this technique to enhance the structure of HBC and make it a more effective carrier for BCNU.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of BCNU on the N-doped HBC
ce:section.ce:para: The molecule NHBC is represented in Fig. 5 with its corresponding MEP map and optimized structure. The central ring of the HBC comprises nitrogen atoms in place of three of its carbon atoms. The geometrical parameters of the structure remain consistent regardless of the basis set level and the equilibrium lengths of bonds 1 to 5 are approximately 1.39 Å, 1.40 Å, 1.46 Å, 1.42 Å, and 1.40 Å, respectively. Due to the presence of nitrogen atoms, the structure exhibits a slightly non-planar, saddle shape. When the lower basis set is utilized the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of NHBC are −3.76 eV and −1.94 eV, respectively, resulting in an E g value of 1.82 eV, which classifies it as a semiconductor. Alternatively, utilizing a higher level of basis set yields E HOMO, E LUMO, and E g values of approximately −3.82, −2.02, and 1.80 eV, respectively.

In order to determine the most stable configuration of the NHBC-BCNU complex, we utilized the electrostatic potential map of drug and nanographene molecules. We optimized all possible NHBC-BCNU initial configurations and conducted stretching frequency calculations. Finally, we discovered that the drug molecule is adsorbed onto the NHBC surface with a minimum interaction distance of 2.9 Å between the O14 atom of the drug and one of the NHBC nitrogen atoms (Fig. 6 (a)). The resulting E ads values were calculated to be −17.0 and −17.3 kcal.mol−1 using lower and higher theoretical levels of basis set, respectively (Table 1). It is notable that the adsorption energy and thermodynamic parameters do not show a noticeable change compared to pristine HBC, and relatively strong interactions occur between NHBC and BCNU. Similarly to the HBC-BCNU case, the geometry of NHBC and BCNU remains almost the same after the interaction, indicating insignificant changes in bond lengths and bond angles despite the strong interaction.

The adsorption of BCNU on the NHBC surface stabilizes the HOMO and LUMO energy levels by approximately 0.11 eV and 0.51 eV, respectively, when using the 6-31+G(d) basis set. This significantly decreases E g from 1.82 eV to 1.41 eV, which equals –22.3 %. Calculations performed with the B3LYP functional and 6-311+G(d,p) basis set indicated that both HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the NHBC stabilized by 0.10 and 0.43 eV, leading to a 18.0 % decrease in E g. Importantly, these results were not affected by the choice of basis set level. The PDOS diagram of the NHBC-BCNU complex shows that NHBC determines the HOMO energy level, while the drug contribute to determining the LUMO energy (Fig. 6(b)). Fig. 7 confirms this, displaying the distribution of HOMO and LUMO orbitals in the NHBC-BCNU structure.

The PDOS diagram and Fig. 7 are supported by Fig. 8 (a), which shows that hydrogen atoms and carbons near nitrogen atoms have a more positive atomic charge than other atoms. Nitrogens and carbon atoms close to hydrogens have the most negative values of partial charges. The partial charge value of edge carbon atoms is the most negative, with values between −0.587 |e| to −0.738 |e|. The atomic charge of NHBC internal carbons is close to zero, and the partial charge of hydrogen atoms ranges from 0.117 |e| to 0.122 |e|. After the interaction of NHBC and BCNU, the atomic charges on the nanographene surface are more uniformly distributed (Fig. 8 (b)). However, the type of atoms with the highest and lowest partial charges remains the same. NBO calculations show that NHBC transfers a charge amount of 0.13 |e| to the drug molecule.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of BCNU on the BN-doped HBC
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen doping did not affect the adsorption energy of BCNU on the surface of nanographene, but it improved drug detection. In the next step, we replaced the remaining three carbon atoms in the central ring of NHBC with boron atoms, which have more metallic properties to obtain the structure of BNHBC (as shown in Fig. 9 ). The structure of BNHBC nanographene contains six types of bonds, marked as numbers 1 to 6 in the figure, with an average length of about 1.39 Å, 1.40 Å, 1.48 Å, 1.51 Å, 1.42 Å and 1.45 Å, respectively. Again, the geometrical parameters are not affected by choosing a higher level of basis set. The energy levels of the frontier molecular orbitals (HOMO and LUMO) of BNHBC have been calculated to be −5.76 eV and −1.84 eV, respectively, using the B3LYP functional and 6-31+G(d) basis set, resulting in an E g of 3.92 eV (refer to Table 1). When utilizing the 6-311+G(d,p) basis set, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels are calculated to be approximately −5.82 eV and −1.93 eV, giving rise to an E g of 3.89 eV. The most stable BNHBC-BCNU complex is shown in Fig. 10 , along with the partial density of states (PDOS) diagram that displays the contribution of each BCNU and BNHBC molecule to the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the complex. The adsorption energy of BCNU on the BNHBC nanographene surface for this configuration at 298 K and 1 atm is −17.9 and −19.1 kcal.mol−1, for the lower and higher theoretical levels, respectively.

Based on our analysis, the interaction energies of the BNHBC-BCNU complex are similar to those of the HBC-BCNU and NHBC-BCNU complexes. Therefore, we explore the changes in the energy levels of the frontier orbitals. Specifically, the E HOMO value of BNHBC increases from −5.76 eV to −5.87 eV, and the LUMO energy level decreases from −1.84 eV to −2.37 eV upon the formation of the BNHBC-BCNU complex. The stabilization of the HOMO and LUMO orbitals leads to a 10.6 % reduction in the HOMO-LUMO energy gap. Using the B3LYP/6-311+G(d,p) calculations, we found a 8.3 % reduction in E g, which corresponds with the results from the lower-level basis set. Although there is not much change in the adsorption energies, enthalpy changes, and Gibbs free energy levels when compared to NHBC, the electronic properties of BNHBC are less sensitive to the presence of BCNU in the environment than NHBC.

The noncovalent interaction (NCI) method, also referred to as the reduced density gradient (RDG) method, is a well-established technique that is commonly used to investigate weak interactions including van der Waals, hydrogen bond, and π-π stacking interactions. These interactions play a crucial role in the formation of chemical bonds [50]. The theory of the NCI method states that the strength and nature of noncovalent interactions are determined by the product sign of the second eigenvalue of diagonalized Hessian matrix sign (λ 2) and electron density ρ( r ). The definition of the RDG function that is expressed in a dimensionless form of electron density gradient norm function, is as follows:
(4)
R D G
r
=
1
2
( 3
π
2
)
1 / 3
∇ ρ
r
ρ
r
4 / 3
To visualize the weak interaction between BCNU and nanographenes, the RDG isosurface value was set to 0.5 a.u as it is suitable for this purpose. The green isosurface in Fig. 13 clearly represents the weak interaction region.

Van der Waals interaction regions always have very small ρ, while the regions corresponding to strong steric effect or evident attractive weak interaction (e.g., hydrogen bond and halogen bond) always have relatively large ρ. Thus, we can define a real space function sign(λ 2)ρ, namely the product of sign of λ 2 and ρ. From the RDG isosurface plot as elucidated in Fig. 13, when sign
(
λ 2
)
ρ
(
r
)
< 0
reflects strong attraction, while van der Waals interaction occurs when sign
(
λ 2
)
ρ
(
r
)
≈ 0
. The steric effect appears when the sign
λ 2
ρ
r
> 0
[51]. In the context of RDG isosurface plots, the spikes observed in the graphs indicate that van der Waals and repulsion forces (such as steric effects in the ring) are the dominant interactions between BCNU and nanographenes.

One of the important issues in developing nanocarriers and biosensors is ensuring the right strength of interaction between the receptor and the drug it holds. If the interactions are too weak, the drug can be released outside the target cells, causing damage to healthy tissues. Conversely, if the interaction is too strong, the drug may not be released at the right time and place, while in the case of sensors, excessive interaction can cause irreparable damage. The recovery time for desorption of adsorbate is calculated using the transition state theory equation:
(5)
τ =
υ
0
- 1
e x p
- E
ads
/ k T
Here, k represents Boltzmann's constant, which is equal to 1.99 × 10-3 kcal.mol−1.K-1and ν 0 is the attempt frequency. According to Eq. (5), a more negative absorption energy leads to a longer recovery time. At room temperature with 6-31+G(d) as the basis set, the time required to release BCNU from HBC, NHBC, and BNHBC is approximately 3.3 s, 1.8 s, and 13.0 s, respectively. The obtained values are 13.0 s, 4.6 s, and 96.0 s, respectively, for the higher level of calculations. The BCNU recovery time appears to be reasonable for all nanographenes, but NHBC has the highest sensitivity for drug detection.

The aim of this research was to explore the feasibility of using hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene (HBC) to transport the drug carmustine (BCNU) with the help of density functional theory (DFT). To achieve this, we carried out various calculations, including geometry optimizations, vibrational frequencies, natural bond orbitals (NBO), and analysis of frontier molecular orbitals (HOMO and LUMO), using functional B3LYP along with the 6-31+G(d) and 6-311+G(d,p) basis sets. We used the molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) map of both the drug and nanographene to identify the most stable configuration of the nanographene-drug complex. Our results show that strong interactions occur between nanographenes and BCNU. Despite these strong interactions, the bond lengths and bond angles of (N, BN)HBC and BCNU remain almost unchanged. The energy gap between HOMO and LUMO (E g) of HBC reduces by only about 6.2 % and 4.6 % upon complexation, obtained from the 6-31+G(d) and 6-311+G(d,p) basis sets, respectively. Therefore, the electronic properties of HBC are not sensitive to the presence of BCNU. The partial density of states (PDOS) plot shows that HBC determines the HOMO energy level in the complex, while both nanographene and drug molecules play a role in determining the LUMO energy level. Our NBO calculations show that only a small amount of charge transfer occurs from HBC to the drug molecule. However, the electronic properties are not favorable from a drug delivery point of view, so we used the doping technique to modify the nanographene’s structure. The results of our calculations show that nitrogen doping does not change the adsorption energy while E g decreases by about 22.3 % and 18.0 %, respectively, when using 6-31+G(d) and 6-311+G(d,p) basis sets. Additionally, the geometry changes of NHBC and BCNU after interaction are insignificant. The PDOS diagram of the NHBC-BCNU complex shows that NHBC determines the HOMO energy level, while the drug contribute to determining the LUMO energy. We also explored the potential of BNHBC-BCNU as a nanocarrier. Our calculations showed that the adsorption energy of BCNU on the BNHBC nanographene surface for this configuration at 298 K and 1 atm is −17.9 and −19.1 kcal.mol−1, for the lower and higher theoretical levels, respectively. The electronic properties of BNHBC are more sensitive to the presence of BCNU than the pristine HBC but it is not as powerful as NHBC for detection of the drug molecule. The PDOS plot shows that BCNU plays a significant role in determining the LUMO energy level of BNHBC-BCNU. The distribution of atomic charges in the BNHBC molecule before and after forming a complex with the drug molecule is similar to NHBC, with the difference that the partial charge of nitrogen atoms is about −0.98 |e|. As expected, the partial charge of boron atoms is strongly positive and about 1.05 |e|. Finally, we investigated the time required to release BCNU from HBC, NHBC, and BNHBC. At room temperature with 6-31+G(d) as the basis set, the time required to release BCNU from HBC, NHBC, and BNHBC is approximately 3.3 s, 1.8 s, and 13.0 s, respectively. The obtained values are 13.0 s, 4.6 s, and 96.0 s, respectively, for the higher level of calculations. It seems that all nanographenes are suitable from a recovery time point of view, but NHBC is the most sensitive for detecting drugs.
Not applicable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Code availability (software application or custom code)
ce:section.ce:para: All calculations were performed using Gaussian 09 software package.

M. Salehpour and Z. Saadati, (both Corresponding author) designed the model, developed the theory and the computational framework and analyzed the data, wrote the manuscript. Amin Amiri Dolatabad performed the computations. All authors provided critical feedback and helped shape the research, analysis and manuscript.

Amin Amiri Dolatabad: Software, Methodology, Data curation. Mahboobeh Salehpour: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zohreh Saadati: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2024.125206.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
